translations.global_feeding_disclaimer: This guide provides general feeding information based on AAP, WHO, and USDA guidelines. It is not a substitute for your pediatrician's advice. Always consult your child's doctor for personalised feeding guidance.
translations.mf_bf_dur_0_3m:
10–45 minutes per feed
Until baby seems satisfied
translations.mf_bf_dur_note_0_3m: Duration varies widely in the newborn period. Feed until baby releases the breast spontaneously or falls asleep.
translations.mf_bf_dur_note_4_6m: Efficient feeders at this age may feed in as little as 5–10 minutes on each side.
translations.mf_bf_dur_note_6_9m: Milk is still the primary nutrition. Offer breast before solid foods at every meal.
translations.mf_ff_amount_note_0_3m: Never force your baby to finish the bottle. Responsive bottle feeding is as important as responsive breastfeeding.
translations.mf_ff_amount_note_4_6m: Amounts increase as baby grows. Follow your baby's hunger and fullness cues — the amounts listed are guides, not targets.
translations.mf_ff_amount_note_6_9m: Reduce formula as solids increase, but milk remains the primary nutrition until 12 months.
translations.mf_ff_type_note: Use iron-fortified infant formula for the first 12 months. Cow's milk is not appropriate as a main drink before 12 months. Soy formula may be recommended for lactose intolerance or galactosaemia — discuss with your doctor.
translations.mf_ff_type_note_9_12m: Continue iron-fortified infant formula until 12 months. At 12 months, transition to whole cow's milk.
translations.mf_ff_type_note_12m: Switch to whole (full-fat) cow's milk at 12 months. Use full-fat until age 2.
translations.mf_switch_lowfat_after_2yr: Switch to low-fat or skimmed milk after age 2 years, or as advised by your doctor.
translations.mf_cue_excited_food: Getting excited when food is prepared
translations.mf_cue_pointing_food: Pointing at food or opening mouth
translations.mf_cue_verbal_ask: Verbally asking for food or milk
translations.mf_cue_late_warning: Tip: Respond to early cues — a crying baby is already overtired from hunger and harder to feed.
translations.mf_full_turns_head: Turns head away from breast or bottle
translations.mf_full_closes_mouth: Closes mouth or keeps lips together
translations.mf_full_throws_food: Throws food off the high chair tray
translations.mf_bf_tip_on_demand: Feed on demand, not by the clock. Breastfed babies self-regulate intake perfectly — your role is to respond, not schedule.
translations.mf_bf_tip_both_sides: Offer both sides at each feed. Let baby fully drain the first side before switching — this ensures the fat-rich hindmilk is received.
translations.mf_bf_tip_latch: A deep latch is essential: baby's mouth should cover the nipple AND a large portion of the areola. A shallow latch causes pain and poor milk transfer.
translations.mf_bf_tip_wet_diapers: A well-fed newborn produces 6+ wet diapers per day after day 5. This is the clearest sign that milk intake is adequate.
translations.mf_bf_tip_solid_readiness_watch: At 4–6 months, watch for solid food readiness signs — but do not start solids before 6 months unless specifically advised by your doctor.
translations.mf_bf_tip_cluster_evening: Evening cluster feeding (feeding every 30–60 minutes for a few hours) is normal at this age and does not mean your milk supply is low.
translations.mf_bf_tip_milk_first_6_9m: Always offer breast before solid foods until 12 months. Milk provides most calories and nutrition. Solids complement, not replace, milk at this stage.
translations.mf_bf_tip_solids_complement: Think of solids as 'practice' at this stage — textures, flavours, and motor skills. Milk is still the primary nutrition.
translations.mf_bf_tip_iron_rich_first: Breast milk is low in iron after 6 months. Prioritise iron-rich first foods: pureed meat, iron-fortified cereals, lentils.
translations.mf_bf_tip_milk_secondary_9m: From 9 months, solid meals become increasingly nutritionally significant. Milk feeds shift from primary nutrition to secondary.
translations.mf_bf_tip_cup_transition: Begin offering expressed milk or water in an open cup or straw cup from 6 months to build cup-drinking skills before the 12-month bottle transition.
translations.mf_bf_tip_toddler_who_ok: The WHO recommends breastfeeding alongside solid foods for up to 2 years and beyond, as long as both mother and child wish to continue.
translations.mf_bf_tip_toddler_nutrition_supplement: From 12 months, ensure toddler's nutritional needs are met primarily through solid foods. Breast milk is a valuable supplement.
translations.mf_bf_tip_toddler_who_ok_extended: Extended breastfeeding (beyond 2 years) is safe and continues to provide immunity and comfort. The WHO supports it. Gradual, child-led weaning is a valid choice.
translations.mf_bf_tip_mutual_weaning: When you choose to wean, do so gradually — drop one feed per week. This protects your milk supply from engorgement and supports your toddler's emotional adjustment.
translations.mf_bf_warn_no_water_0_6m: Warning: Do not give water to babies under 6 months. Their kidneys cannot process excess water, and it can cause dangerous sodium dilution (hyponatraemia).
translations.mf_bf_warn_no_juice_0_6m: Warning: No juice before 12 months. No herbal teas, no glucose water. Breast milk or formula only for the first 6 months.
translations.mf_bf_warn_no_solids_before_6m: The AAP recommends exclusive breastfeeding for around 6 months before introducing solids. Starting before 4 months is associated with increased obesity risk.
translations.mf_bf_warn_milk_main_6_12m: Medical note: Breast milk must remain the primary nutrition from 6–12 months. Solids at this stage complement, not replace, milk feeds.
translations.mf_ff_tip_prepare_fresh: Prepare formula fresh at each feed where possible. Prepared formula can be refrigerated for up to 24 hours but not reheated more than once.
translations.mf_ff_tip_discard_1hr: Discard any formula left in the bottle after 1 hour. Bacteria from saliva multiply rapidly in partially consumed formula at room temperature.
translations.mf_ff_tip_responsive: Practice paced bottle feeding: hold the bottle horizontally, pause every few minutes to allow baby to regulate intake — just like breastfeeding.
translations.mf_ff_tip_burp: Burp your baby halfway through the feed and at the end. Formula-fed babies tend to take in more air than breastfed babies.
translations.mf_ff_tip_milk_before_solids: Always offer formula before solid foods at meals until 12 months. Milk is the primary nutrition — solids are supplementary.
translations.mf_ff_tip_sippy_cup_intro: Begin introducing an open or straw cup at 6 months. This builds the motor skill needed to transition off bottles at 12 months.
translations.mf_ff_tip_cup_transition_9m: Begin phasing in cup feeds to replace one bottle per day. Target: all milk from a cup by 12–14 months.
translations.mf_ff_tip_no_bottle_bed: Never put your baby to bed with a bottle. Milk pooling in the mouth overnight causes early childhood tooth decay.
translations.mf_ff_tip_transition_cows_milk_at_12m: At 12 months, transition from infant formula to whole cow's milk. Do this gradually over 1–2 weeks: mix increasing proportions of cow's milk with formula.
translations.mf_ff_tip_no_bottle_12m: The AAP recommends weaning off bottles entirely by 12–15 months. Prolonged bottle use is associated with dental decay and over-consumption of milk.
translations.mf_ff_tip_cup_only_12m: Serve all milk from a cup from 12 months. If your child is resistant, transition gradually — replace one bottle at a time.
translations.mf_ff_tip_max_500ml_cows_milk: Limit cow's milk to 480 ml (16 oz) per day for toddlers. More than this reduces appetite for iron-rich foods and can cause iron-deficiency anaemia.
translations.mf_ff_tip_cup_only_18m: All milk should be served in a cup by 18 months. If still using a bottle, begin a firm transition plan.
translations.mf_ff_warn_no_cows_milk: Warning: Do not use cow's milk as a main drink before 12 months. Its protein and mineral content is not suitable for infant kidneys and it lacks adequate iron.
translations.mf_ff_warn_no_dilute: Warning: Never dilute formula with extra water. This reduces calorie and nutrient concentration and can cause hyponatraemia (sodium deficiency) — which can be fatal.
translations.mf_ff_warn_no_homemade: Warning: Never use homemade formula recipes. They cannot reliably meet infant nutritional needs and have caused serious infant illness and death.
translations.mf_ff_warn_no_lowfat_under_2yr: Warning: Use full-fat (whole) cow's milk from 12–24 months. Low-fat or skimmed milk lacks the fat needed for brain development at this age.
translations.mf_combo_desc_0_3m: Combination feeding (breastfeeding and formula supplementation) can be a good choice for families where exclusive breastfeeding is not possible. Introduce bottles at 4–6 weeks to allow breastfeeding to be well established first.
translations.mf_combo_desc_4_6m: Combination feeding is well-established by this age. Alternate between breast and bottle as suits your family's schedule.
translations.mf_combo_desc_6_9m: As solids are introduced, combination feeding continues alongside. Ensure milk remains the primary nutrition — solids are supplementary.
translations.mf_combo_desc_9_12m: Combination feeding works well alongside solid meals. Begin cup-feeding skills now for the 12-month bottle transition.
translations.mf_combo_desc_12_18m: From 12 months, transition to whole cow's milk in a cup, offered at mealtimes. Breastfeeds can continue as desired.
translations.mf_combo_desc_18_36m: Any milk source — breast, cow's milk, or fortified plant milk (check with your doctor) — in a cup alongside solid meals.
translations.mf_combo_tip_paced_bottle: Use paced bottle feeding to mimic the breast: slow flow nipple, horizontal bottle position, frequent pauses. This prevents over-feeding and supports breastfeeding continuation.
translations.mf_combo_tip_maintain_supply: To maintain breast milk supply alongside formula, breastfeed or pump at least as often as formula is given. Milk supply responds to demand.
translations.mf_combo_tip_slow_flow_nipple: Use a slow-flow bottle nipple to match breast milk flow and reduce nipple preference issues.
translations.mf_combo_tip_timing: Offer bottle at times when breastfeeding is not possible (work, separation). Nurse when available. This natural patterning protects supply.
translations.mf_combo_warn_nipple_confusion: Note: Some babies (not all) show nipple preference after early bottle introduction. If you notice reluctance at the breast, consult a lactation consultant.
translations.mf_combo_tip_milk_before_solids: Offer milk before solid foods at every meal. Milk is still the primary nutrition.
translations.mf_combo_tip_cup_both_types: Introduce a cup for both breast milk and formula at 6–9 months to build cup skills before 12 months.
translations.mf_combo_tip_cup_only_12m: Serve all formula or cow's milk from a cup from 12 months. Breastfeeds can continue.
translations.mf_combo_tip_cup_only_18m: All milk from cups by 18 months.
translations.mf_transition_formula_to_cows_12m: At 12 months, infant formula is no longer needed. Transition to whole cow's milk over 1–2 weeks by mixing increasing proportions.
translations.sf_ready_6m_note: The AAP recommends introducing solids around 6 months. Most babies show readiness signs between 6–7 months.
translations.sf_readiness_signs_label: Signs of readiness for solid foods
translations.sf_sign_head_control: Holds head steady and upright independently
translations.sf_sign_sits_with_support: Sits with minimal support (high chair with support is fine)
translations.sf_sign_shows_interest: Shows clear interest in food — reaches for it, opens mouth when food approaches
translations.sf_sign_tongue_thrust_gone: Tongue-thrust reflex has reduced (no longer automatically pushing food out)
translations.sf_sign_doubles_birth_weight: Has approximately doubled birth weight (typically around 6 months)
translations.sf_readiness_not_before_4m: Do not start solids before 4 months under any circumstances. Before 4 months, the gut is not ready and the tongue-thrust reflex is strong.
translations.sf_tip_watch_not_4m: Watch for readiness signs from around 5.5 months, but do not act before 6 months unless specifically recommended by your doctor.
translations.sf_purees_desc_6_9m: Begin with single-ingredient smooth purees to introduce individual flavours and check for reactions. Progress to thicker, lumpier textures over weeks.
translations.sf_purees_desc_9_12m: Move from smooth purees to mashed and minced textures. Lumps are important — they develop chewing skills essential for speech and dental development.
translations.sf_texture_lumpy_9m: Mashed and minced — some soft lumps are important
translations.sf_texture_soft_dissolvable: Soft, easy to squish between fingers
translations.sf_texture_varied_12m: Varied textures from family foods, chopped to safe size
translations.sf_consistency_6m: Purees should be smooth enough to run off the spoon without clumping. Progress to thicker textures within 2–4 weeks.
translations.sf_consistency_9m: Progress from smooth to lumpy mashed. By 9–10 months, introduce soft minced and chopped pieces. Delay in introducing lumps is associated with feeding difficulties at 2 years (Coulthard et al.).
translations.sf_iron_cereals_note: Mix with breast milk or formula to a smooth consistency. A good first iron source.
translations.sf_pureed_meat_note: The best source of haem iron for breastfed babies. Cook thoroughly and blend with liquid to smooth consistency.
translations.sf_pureed_veg_name: Pureed vegetables (sweet potato, butternut, peas, courgette)
translations.sf_pureed_veg_note: Introduce vegetables before fruits to avoid sweet preference. Offer each vegetable 8–10 times before concluding rejection.
translations.sf_pureed_fruit_note: No added sugar needed. Ripe fruit is sweet enough naturally. Offer alongside vegetables for balance.
translations.sf_pureed_lentil_note: Excellent plant-based iron source. Pair with vitamin C-rich foods (tomato, sweet pepper) to boost non-haem iron absorption.
translations.sf_yogurt_note: Use plain, unsweetened, full-fat yogurt. Safe as a food from 6 months even though cow's milk as a drink is not appropriate until 12 months. Rich in calcium and probiotics.
translations.sf_serving_start_6m: Start with 1–2 teaspoons per meal. Increase gradually to 2–4 tablespoons over 4–6 weeks. Follow baby's cues.
translations.sf_mashed_veg_note: Cook until fork-tender and mash with a fork. Soft lumps are appropriate and important for skill development.
translations.sf_soft_fruit_name: Soft, ripe fruit (banana, ripe pear, ripe mango)
translations.sf_soft_fruit_note: Mash or cut into small soft pieces. Avoid hard raw fruits.
translations.sf_minced_meat_name: Minced or finely chopped cooked meat
translations.sf_minced_meat_note: Cook thoroughly. Mince finely or shred. Moisten with broth or sauce to prevent choking.
translations.sf_soft_egg_name: Softly scrambled or mashed hard-boiled egg
translations.sf_soft_egg_note: Introduce early — by 6 months — to reduce allergy risk (AAP 2023 LEAP guidelines).
translations.sf_soft_cheese_name: Soft, mild cheese (ricotta, cream cheese, cheddar)
translations.sf_soft_cheese_note: Use pasteurised cheese only. Avoid blue cheese and soft-rind cheeses (Brie, Camembert).
translations.sf_finger_foods_desc_9m: Finger foods develop pincer grasp, chewing, and self-feeding skills. Start with soft, easy-to-dissolve pieces.
translations.sf_finger_foods_desc_12m: Toddlers can manage a wide variety of finger foods from the family table, chopped or prepared to safe size.
translations.sf_finger_food_readiness_9m: Readiness signs: sitting upright well, bringing objects to mouth, showing interest in self-feeding.
translations.ff_prep_dice_small: Cook until very soft. Dice into pea-sized pieces.
translations.ff_prep_cook_very_soft: Cook until very soft (overcook by normal standards). Cut to safe size.
translations.ff_prep_small_pieces: Cut into small, safe pieces (pea-sized to thumb-length strips)
translations.ff_prep_soft_cooked: Cook until no runny whites. Serve in soft clumps.
translations.ff_prep_soft_cubes: Cut into pea-sized cubes. Silken tofu is best at this stage.
translations.ff_prep_dice_fork_tender: Cook until fork-tender. Dice into pea-sized pieces.
translations.ff_prep_small_dice: Peel, remove seeds if applicable, dice to pea-size
translations.ff_prep_any_style: Any preparation — scrambled, boiled, fried (no oil). Cut into pieces.
translations.ff_prep_quarter_lengthwise: Quarter lengthwise. Never serve whole grapes under 4 years.
translations.ff_prep_cooked_soft: Cook until very soft and mashable.
translations.ff_safety_thumb_finger_size: Food pieces should be no larger than a thumbnail or an adult's little fingernail
translations.ff_safety_soft_enough: Food should squish easily between two fingers — if it doesn't, it is not soft enough
translations.ff_safety_supervised: Always supervise eating. Never leave a baby or toddler alone with food.
translations.ff_safety_upright_position: Always seat baby upright in a high chair. Never feed a reclined or lying-down baby.
translations.sf_family_foods_desc_12m: From 12 months, toddlers can share most family foods, prepared safely (low salt, low sugar, appropriate texture).
translations.sf_family_foods_desc_18m: From 18 months, toddlers eat full family meals with appropriate modifications — cut to safe sizes, low salt, low sugar.
translations.sf_family_foods_modify_note: Modify family food by: removing baby's portion before adding salt or seasoning; cutting to safe sizes; mashing or chopping harder items.
translations.sf_family_foods_full_participation: Full participation at the family table. Same food as the family with minimal modification.
translations.sf_portion_toddler_note: Toddler portion = 1 tablespoon per year of age per food. A 2-year-old needs about 2 tablespoons of each food per meal — their stomach is the size of their fist.
translations.sf_ff_low_salt_family: Remove toddler's portion before adding salt or sauces high in salt (soy sauce, ketchup)
translations.sf_ff_low_sugar_family: Avoid sweetened foods and drinks. Offer fruit as the primary sweet flavour.
translations.sf_ff_shared_mealtimes: Eat together as a family whenever possible. Children learn eating behaviours by watching caregivers.
translations.sf_ff_no_pressure: Do not pressure, bribe, or force eating. This increases food refusal and negative mealtime associations.
translations.sf_ff_variety_exposure: Keep offering a wide variety of foods even if refused. Research shows acceptance after 15–20 exposures on average.
translations.sf_tip_single_ingredient: Introduce one new food at a time and wait 3–5 days before introducing another. This makes it easy to identify any allergic reactions.
translations.sf_tip_new_food_wait_3_5_days: Wait 3–5 days between each new food. Watch for rash, vomiting, diarrhoea, or breathing changes.
translations.sf_tip_iron_rich_first: Prioritise iron-rich foods as first solids: fortified cereals, pureed meat, lentils. Breast milk is low in iron after 6 months and iron stores from birth begin to deplete.
translations.sf_tip_no_added_salt_sugar: Never add salt or sugar to baby foods. Baby kidneys cannot process excess salt, and added sugar promotes harmful habits.
translations.sf_tip_texture_progress: Progress textures gradually from smooth to lumpy to chopped. Sticking with smooth purees too long delays chewing skill development.
translations.sf_tip_watch_not_4m: Watch for readiness signs but do not introduce solids before 6 months.
translations.sf_tip_blw_intro: Baby-led weaning (BLW) — offering soft finger foods from the start instead of purees — is safe when readiness signs are present and safe food preparation is followed.
translations.sf_tip_gagging_normal: Gagging is a normal and protective reflex. It is not choking. A gagging baby makes noise and moves food forward. A choking baby is silent. Learn infant choking first aid.
translations.sf_tip_lumpier_textures: Progress from smooth to lumpy by 9–10 months. Research shows that babies still on smooth purees at 10 months are significantly more likely to be picky eaters at age 2.
translations.sf_tip_family_food_adapted: Begin offering adapted family foods alongside purees from 9–10 months. This eases the transition to full family meals at 12 months.
translations.sf_tip_self_feeding_12m: Offer finger foods and a spoon at every meal from 12 months. Self-feeding is messy but essential for developing independence and a healthy relationship with food.
translations.sf_tip_3_meals_2_snacks: From 12 months, aim for 3 meals and 2 snacks per day. Structured meal times prevent grazing, which reduces appetite at proper mealtimes.
translations.sf_tip_division_of_responsibility: Ellyn Satter's Division of Responsibility: Your job is to decide WHAT is offered, WHEN, and WHERE. Your toddler's job is to decide IF they eat and HOW MUCH. Do not override their role.
translations.sf_tip_no_short_order_cook: Do not cook separate meals for a picky toddler. Offer one family meal. Include at least one food you know they like, but do not make a separate 'safe' meal.
translations.sf_tip_repeated_exposure_15_20: Research shows that children need an average of 15–20 exposures to a new food before accepting it. Offer without pressure — tasting is optional.
translations.sf_tip_positive_mealtime: Keep mealtimes positive and pressure-free. A calm, social mealtime is the single most effective environment for broadening a toddler's food acceptance.
translations.sf_warn_no_honey_purees: Warning: No honey in any form — including in recipes and baked goods — before 12 months. Honey can contain Clostridium botulinum spores which cause infant botulism. Infant botulism can be fatal.
translations.sf_warn_choking_thin_purees: Note: Thin, runny purees can occasionally cause coughing in young babies. Ensure the consistency is appropriate for your baby's age and skill.
translations.sf_warn_choking_9m: Warning: Always supervise eating. Review safe food preparation guidelines for this age. Never leave a baby alone with food.
translations.sf_warn_choking_finger_foods: Warning: Always supervise finger food eating. Be familiar with the difference between gagging (normal, noisy) and choking (silent, requires action). Learn infant choking first aid.
translations.sf_warn_no_whole_grapes: Warning: Never serve whole grapes, cherry tomatoes, or similarly round foods without cutting lengthwise into quarters. Whole round foods are a leading cause of infant and toddler choking deaths.
translations.sf_warn_no_raw_hard_veg: Warning: No raw hard vegetables (raw carrot, celery) for babies and toddlers under 3 years. Cook until very soft.
translations.sf_warn_choking_12m: Warning: Choking risk remains high through toddlerhood. Continue safe food preparation: quarter grapes lengthwise, cut round foods, avoid whole nuts.
translations.sf_warn_picky_eating_normal: Note: Picky eating is extremely common from 18 months to 3 years — a developmental phase, not a problem. A child eating fewer than 20 foods or showing extreme distress around food should be assessed by a feeding therapist.
translations.sched_combo_bf_or_milk: Breastfeed or cow's milk in cup
translations.sched_cows_milk_120_180ml: Whole cow's milk: 120–180 ml (4–6 oz) in cup
translations.sched_cows_milk_120ml: Whole cow's milk: 120 ml (4 oz) in cup
translations.sched_solid_breakfast_6m: Solids: 2–4 tbsp iron-rich puree (cereal, meat, or lentil) + vegetable or fruit
translations.sched_solid_dinner_6m: Solids: 2–4 tbsp fruit or vegetable puree
translations.sched_solid_breakfast_9m: Solids: Iron-fortified cereal + soft fruit + yogurt (2–3 tbsp total)
translations.sched_solid_lunch_9m: Solids: Mashed vegetables + minced protein + soft starch (3–4 tbsp)
translations.sched_solid_dinner_9m: Solids: Mashed family meal (adapted, low salt) — 3–4 tbsp
translations.sched_solid_breakfast_12m: Breakfast: Egg, whole grain toast, soft fruit
translations.sched_solid_lunch_12m: Lunch: Protein + vegetable + starch, family food
translations.sched_solid_breakfast_18m: Breakfast: Family breakfast (egg, porridge, fruit, toast)
translations.sched_solid_lunch_18m: Lunch: Family lunch — protein, vegetable, starch
translations.sched_solid_dinner_18m: Dinner: Family dinner (low salt, cut appropriately)
translations.sched_snack_finger_food: Snack: Soft finger foods (puffs, soft fruit, yogurt)
translations.sched_snack_12m: Snack: Soft fruit, cheese, crackers, yogurt
translations.sched_snack_18m: Snack: Fruit, cheese, vegetable sticks (soft), hummus, yogurt
translations.sched_flexible_disclaimer: This is a sample guide — not a strict schedule. Follow your child's hunger and fullness cues. Times are approximate.
translations.sched_bf_note_0_3m: Newborns feed on demand. This schedule shows average spacing — your baby may feed more or less frequently. Night feeds are expected and normal.
translations.sched_ff_note_0_3m: Formula-fed babies may space feeds slightly further apart than breastfed babies. Never withhold feeds to stretch the schedule.
translations.sched_combo_note_0_3m: Alternate breast and bottle as suits your family. Maintain breastfeeding frequency to protect milk supply.
translations.sched_bf_note_4_6m: Breastfeeds are still frequent. 4-month regression may temporarily disrupt this schedule — maintain routine.
translations.sched_ff_note_4_6m: Formula feeds begin to consolidate. Watch for hunger cues rather than strictly following the clock.
translations.sched_combo_note_4_6m: Combine breast and bottle as your schedule allows.
translations.sched_bf_note_6_9m: Milk is offered before every solid meal. Milk remains the primary nutrition.
translations.sched_ff_note_6_9m: Formula before every solid meal. Reduce formula gradually as solid intake increases — do not drop formula feeds rapidly.
translations.sched_combo_note_6_9m: Either breast or formula before each solid meal.
translations.sched_bf_note_9_12m: 3–5 breastfeeds alongside 3 solid meals and 1 snack per day.
translations.sched_ff_note_9_12m: 3–4 formula feeds alongside 3 solid meals and 1 snack. Begin cup-feeding skills.
translations.sched_combo_note_9_12m: 3–4 milk feeds (breast or formula) alongside 3 meals.
translations.sched_bf_note_12_18m: 2–4 breastfeeds per day alongside 3 meals and 2 snacks. Solid foods are now the primary nutrition.
translations.sched_cows_milk_note_12_18m: 2–3 cups of whole cow's milk per day. Solid foods are the primary nutrition. No bottle from 12 months.
translations.sched_combo_note_12_18m: Breast or cow's milk in cup, 2–3 times per day alongside meals.
translations.sched_bf_note_18_36m: 1–2 breastfeeds per day. Solid meals are the primary nutrition.
translations.sched_cows_milk_note_18_36m: 2 cups of whole cow's milk per day (max 480 ml). Meals are primary nutrition.
translations.sched_combo_note_18_36m: Any milk source in a cup, 2 times per day alongside meals.
translations.fta_honey_reason: Honey may contain Clostridium botulinum spores. In infants, these germinate in the gut and produce a toxin causing infant botulism — a potentially fatal illness. Infant gut flora cannot prevent this the way adult flora can. All honey is unsafe under 12 months.
translations.fta_cows_milk_main_name: Cow's milk as a main drink
translations.fta_cows_milk_main_reason: Cow's milk has too much protein and sodium and too little iron for infants. Its protein can damage immature kidneys. It does not provide adequate nutrition to replace breast milk or formula. Full-fat yogurt and soft cheese are safe as foods from 6 months.
translations.fta_water_excess_reason: Babies under 6 months need only breast milk or formula. Water can displace nutritious milk, reduce intake, and cause dangerous hyponatraemia (low sodium). Juice and all other drinks are also inappropriate.
translations.fta_juice_reason: The AAP recommends no juice before 12 months. Juice displaces nutrient-rich milk, increases cavity risk, and contributes to unhealthy sugar intake patterns.
translations.fta_juice_reason_24m: The AAP recommends limiting juice to 120 ml (4 oz) per day maximum from 12–36 months. Offer whole fruit instead — it provides fibre and nutrients juice lacks.
translations.fta_added_salt_reason: Infant kidneys cannot excrete excess sodium efficiently. Avoid adding salt and avoid high-salt foods (processed foods, most restaurant foods, soy sauce, cheese used excessively).
translations.fta_added_sugar_reason: Added sugar provides no nutritional benefit and establishes preferences for sweet foods early. Avoid sweet biscuits, sweetened yogurts, flavoured cereals, and fruit pouches with added sugar.
translations.fta_whole_nuts_name: Whole nuts and large nut pieces
translations.fta_whole_nuts_reason: Whole nuts are a leading choking hazard in children under 5 years. Finely ground nut butters thinned with water or mixed into purees are safe and recommended from 6 months to reduce allergy risk.
translations.fta_round_grapes_reason: Round foods that exactly fit a child's airway are extremely high-risk for fatal choking. Always quarter grapes and cherry tomatoes lengthwise before serving under 4 years.
translations.fta_raw_carrot_reason: Raw hard vegetables (carrots, celery, raw apple) are a significant choking risk. Cook all vegetables until fork-tender for babies and toddlers.
translations.fta_popcorn_reason: Popcorn is a high choking hazard. The irregular shape and dry texture make it extremely dangerous for children under 4 years.
translations.fta_round_cherry_tomato_reason: Like grapes, whole cherry tomatoes fit exactly in a child's airway. Always halve or quarter before serving.
translations.fta_raw_hard_veg_reason: Cook all root vegetables and firm fruits until soft. Raw pieces are a significant choking hazard for all children under 3.
translations.fta_sugary_drinks_name: Sugary drinks (squash, fizzy drinks, flavoured milk)
translations.fta_sugary_drinks_reason: No sugary drinks before age 5. They contribute to dental decay, obesity, and establish lifelong preferences for very sweet beverages.
translations.fta_lowfat_dairy_reason: Children under 2 years need the fat in whole milk for brain development. Low-fat and skimmed milk are not appropriate as main drinks before 2 years.
translations.fta_choking_foods_reason: Under 4 years, avoid: whole hot dogs, large meat chunks, globs of nut butter (safe when thinned), large marshmallows, hard sweets, raw apple with skin. Cut all foods to safe size.
translations.fta_unpasteurized_reason: Raw milk and unpasteurised juices may contain harmful bacteria (E. coli, Salmonella, Listeria) that cause serious illness in infants and young children. Use only pasteurised products.
translations.alg_peanut_reason_4_6m: Introduce peanut products early. The AAP now recommends introducing peanuts at 4–6 months for babies at high risk of allergy (severe eczema, egg allergy). Always use thinned smooth peanut butter, never whole peanuts.
translations.alg_peanut_reason_6m: The AAP recommends early introduction of peanuts (from 6 months) to reduce allergy risk. Use smooth peanut butter thinned with water or pureed into food. Never whole peanuts.
translations.alg_peanut_reason_12m: Continue regular peanut exposure to maintain tolerance. 2–3 times per week is the recommendation from the LEAP trial.
translations.alg_peanut_continue: Continue offering peanuts 2–3 times per week to maintain tolerance.
translations.alg_egg_reason: Introduce eggs early — from 6 months — to reduce allergy risk. Introduce well-cooked egg first. Once tolerated, all egg preparations are appropriate.
translations.alg_egg_reason_12m: Continue regular egg exposure. Egg is an excellent nutrition source — protein, iron, choline.
translations.alg_tree_nut_reason: Use finely ground nut butters or pastes — never whole or chopped nuts under 5 years. Introduce one nut type at a time. Wait 3–5 days between each new nut type.
translations.alg_fish_reason: Introduce fish from 6 months. Choose lower-mercury fish: salmon, cod, trout, tilapia. Avoid high-mercury fish (shark, swordfish, marlin, king mackerel) under 5 years.
translations.alg_shellfish_reason: Introduce shellfish from 6 months. Cook thoroughly. Ensure it is fresh. Introduce one type at a time.
translations.alg_wheat_reason: Introduce wheat-containing foods from 6 months (iron-fortified wheat cereal, bread). Early introduction is now recommended by the AAP to reduce coeliac risk.
translations.alg_soy_reason: Introduce soy from 6 months (tofu, pureed edamame). Soy allergy is less common than peanut or egg allergy.
translations.alg_sesame_reason: Sesame is now the 9th major allergen in the US (2023). Introduce tahini thinned into purees from 6 months.
translations.alg_early_intro_note: AAP 2023 guidance: Early introduction (before 12 months) of common allergens significantly reduces lifelong allergy risk. Introduce one at a time. Wait 3–5 days. If reaction occurs: hives, vomiting, difficulty breathing — seek immediate medical care.
translations.alg_introduce_early_aap: Introduce early (before 12 months). The AAP recommends not delaying introduction of any allergen. Early, regular exposure is the best prevention.
translations.alg_continue_exposure: Continue offering this food regularly (2–3 times per week) to maintain tolerance.
translations.ftip_demand_feed_body: In the first months, feed whenever your baby shows hunger cues — not on a fixed schedule. Newborns have tiny stomachs (about the size of a marble at birth). Frequent feeding is necessary for adequate nutrition and to establish milk supply.
translations.ftip_burping_body_0_3m: Burp your baby halfway through each feed and at the end. Try: over the shoulder, face-down on your lap, or sitting upright. Some babies need more burping than others. Formula-fed babies typically need more burping than breastfed babies.
translations.ftip_growth_spurts_body: Growth spurts typically occur at 2–3 weeks, 6 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months. During growth spurts, your baby may feed much more frequently — this is normal. Breastfeeding mothers: increased feeding directly increases milk supply. Do not supplement with formula unless medically necessary.
translations.ftip_weight_gain_body_0_3m: Newborns lose up to 7–10% of birth weight in the first few days (normal). They should regain birth weight by 10–14 days. From then: 150–200 g per week for the first 3 months. Contact your doctor if weight gain is consistently below this.
translations.ftip_vitamin_d_body: The AAP recommends all breastfed babies receive 400 IU of vitamin D daily from the first few days of life. Formula contains vitamin D, but formula-fed babies need supplementation if they consume less than 1 litre per day. Discuss with your doctor.
translations.ftip_solid_readiness_title: When is my baby ready for solids?
translations.ftip_solid_readiness_body: Look for ALL of these signs together: holds head steady, sits with support, has lost the tongue-thrust reflex, shows interest in food. Age alone is not sufficient — wait for signs of readiness. Most babies show readiness around 6 months.
translations.ftip_4m_regression_body: Around 4 months, babies become more distractible during feeds — shorter, more frequent feeds are common. This coincides with the 4-month sleep regression. Maintain feeding frequency and respond to hunger cues.
translations.ftip_milk_first_body: From 6–12 months, always offer breast milk or formula before solid foods at every meal. Milk provides over 70% of caloric and nutritional needs at this stage. Solids are for learning and supplementation — not to replace milk.
translations.ftip_allergy_intro_body: The 2023 AAP guidelines recommend introducing all major allergens (peanut, egg, fish, wheat, soy, sesame, shellfish, tree nuts) before 12 months. Introduce one new allergen at a time with 3–5 days between. Start with peanut and egg. If your baby has severe eczema or a known egg allergy, consult your doctor before introducing peanuts.
translations.ftip_iron_body: Iron stores from birth deplete around 6 months. Breast milk is low in iron. Prioritise iron-rich foods: meat, poultry, fish, iron-fortified cereals, lentils, beans. Pair plant iron sources with vitamin C (tomato, sweet pepper, orange) to boost absorption. Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional deficiency in children under 2.
translations.ftip_iron_body_9m: By 9 months, iron-rich foods at every meal are essential. Serve red meat or dark poultry at least 3 times per week, or lentils/beans daily alongside a vitamin C source.
translations.ftip_gagging_vs_choking_title: Gagging vs. Choking — know the difference
translations.ftip_gagging_vs_choking_body: Gagging is normal and protective. A gagging baby makes noise (retching sounds), goes red in the face, and moves food forward. Do NOT intervene — they are managing it. Choking is silent. A choking baby cannot cry or make sound, may be going blue, and requires immediate first aid. Every parent should complete an infant choking first aid course.
translations.ftip_texture_progress_body: Research by Coulthard et al. shows that babies not offered lumpy foods by 9–10 months are significantly more likely to be picky eaters and have feeding difficulties at age 7. Progress from smooth to lumpy to chopped — even if some gagging occurs. Gagging is learning.
translations.ftip_family_table_body: Eating together as a family is one of the strongest predictors of a child accepting a wide variety of foods. Children learn eating behaviour by watching caregivers. From 9 months, involve your baby in family mealtimes whenever possible.
translations.ftip_cup_intro_body: Introduce an open cup or straw cup from 6 months. The goal is to be fully off bottles by 12–15 months. Prolonged bottle use (beyond 15 months) is associated with dental cavities, excess milk intake, and delayed self-feeding skills.
translations.ftip_food_neophobia_body: Fear of new foods (neophobia) develops strongly between 12–36 months — an evolutionary trait. Research shows that children need 15–20 non-pressured exposures to a new food before accepting it. Keep offering without pressure. Tasting is optional. Rejection is not final.
translations.ftip_division_responsibility_body: Ellyn Satter's Division of Responsibility is the most evidence-backed feeding framework: You decide WHAT food is offered, WHEN meals happen, and WHERE. Your child decides IF they eat and HOW MUCH. Overriding their 'how much' role — by pressuring, bribing, or force-feeding — disrupts appetite self-regulation and is associated with obesity and disordered eating.
translations.ftip_milk_limit_12m_title: Don't over-rely on milk after 12 months
translations.ftip_milk_limit_12m_body: After 12 months, solid foods are the primary nutrition. Limit cow's milk to 480 ml (16 oz) per day. More than this fills the stomach, reduces appetite for iron-rich foods, and causes iron-deficiency anaemia — the most common deficiency in toddlers.
translations.ftip_iron_toddler_body: Toddler iron needs are high (7–10 mg per day). Offer iron-rich foods daily: meat, eggs, beans, lentils, tofu, fortified cereals. Serve with vitamin C-rich foods to boost absorption. Iron-deficiency in toddlers affects brain development, language, and behaviour.
translations.ftip_picky_eating_body: Picky eating peaks between 18 months and 3 years. Strategies that work: division of responsibility, repeated low-pressure exposure, eating together, involving toddlers in food preparation, serving one liked food alongside new foods. Strategies that backfire: pressure, bribing with dessert, making separate meals, disguising foods.
translations.ftip_positive_mealtime_body: A calm, social, pressure-free mealtime is the most important factor in raising an adventurous eater. Turn off screens. Sit together. Talk about things other than the food. Neutral language about food: 'You don't have to eat it, just try if you want.' No: 'Eat your vegetables or no dessert.'
translations.ftip_milk_cap_18m_body: Limit all milk (cow's milk, breast milk, plant milk) to 480 ml (16 oz) per day. Excessive milk is the leading cause of iron-deficiency anaemia in toddlers. If your toddler is drinking more than this and eating poorly, a gradual reduction in milk is often the fastest improvement.
translations.ftip_vitamins_toddler_body: The AAP recommends: Vitamin D (400–600 IU/day) for children not getting regular sun exposure. Iron supplementation (1 mg/kg/day) for children at risk of deficiency — ask your doctor. In the UK, the NHS recommends vitamin A, C, and D drops from 6 months to 5 years. Always discuss supplements with your pediatrician before starting.
